questions.question: Which pillar of OOP is primarily achieved using private fields and public getters/setters?
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides internal state and exposes controlled access.

questions.question: What is printed?
questions.explanation: Runtime type is Dog; overridden speak prints D.

questions.question: Which keyword inherits from a superclass in Java?
questions.explanation: classes use extends; implements is for interfaces.

questions.question: Which constructor call is implicit in a child class if none is written?
questions.explanation: Compiler inserts super() unless another super(...) is present.

questions.question: Can a class extend more than one class in Java?
questions.explanation: Java classes have single inheritance; multiple interface implementation is allowed.

questions.question: What happens?
questions.explanation: Only P(int) exists; compiler does not add default constructor.

questions.question: An interface declares void draw();. A concrete class implements it but provides no draw. What happens?
questions.explanation: Concrete classes must implement all interface methods.

questions.question: Which modifier prevents a class from being subclassed?
questions.explanation: final on a class blocks extension.

questions.question: Why hide fields as private?
options: To enforce validation and invariants at access points
questions.explanation: Encapsulation protects object state and allows controlled mutation.

questions.question: Which is proper encapsulation for a balance field?
options: private double balance; public double getBalance() { return balance; }
questions.explanation: Private field with accessor exposes read without uncontrolled write.

questions.question: What is wrong with: public class User { public String password; }
options: Password should be exposed via println only
Sensitive data should not be public fields
questions.explanation: Public fields break encapsulation and leak mutable state.

questions.question: JavaBeans-style mutator naming convention is:
questions.explanation: Setters use setX; getters use getX or isX for boolean.

questions.question: A class has private int count; and only increment(). Can external code set count directly?
options: Yes, via reflection only in all JDKs
No, without a setter or public field
questions.explanation: Without accessible mutators, external code cannot assign private fields legally.

questions.question: A setter validates age and throws if negative. This illustrates:
questions.explanation: Centralizing validation in setters is classic encapsulation.

questions.question: Package-private (no modifier) visibility means:
options: Visible only within the same package
questions.explanation: Default access is package scope.

questions.question: A Car has an Engine. This relationship is best described as:
questions.explanation: Composition: Car contains an Engine reference.

questions.question: Which design expresses is-a?
questions.explanation: extends models specialization: ElectricCar is a Car.

questions.question: Liskov substitution means:
options: A subclass instance can be used wherever the superclass type is expected
A superclass can replace subclass always
questions.explanation: Subtypes must honor the superclass contract when substituted.

questions.question: Given class A { } class B extends A { } — which assignment compiles?
questions.explanation: Subclass instance is assignable to superclass reference.

questions.question: Team has many Members but Member is not a Team. Best relationship?
questions.explanation: has-a via collection models containment without incorrect is-a.

questions.question: Aggregation differs from strong composition mainly because:
options: Child lifecycle may be independent of container
questions.explanation: In aggregation, parts can exist outside the whole.

questions.question: What prints?
questions.explanation: Runtime dispatch invokes Circle.draw.

questions.question: An abstract class can:
questions.explanation: Abstract classes may mix abstract and concrete members.

questions.question: Which is valid?
options: abstract class W { abstract void work(); } W x = new W();
class J extends W { void work(){} } W x = new J();
questions.explanation: Concrete J implements work; can assign to W reference.

questions.question: Pre-Java 8 interface instance methods were implicitly:
questions.explanation: Implicitly public abstract (except static/default added in Java 8).

questions.question: What prints?
questions.explanation: Default method hi inherited prints I.

questions.question: Method overriding requires:
options: Same name, compatible parameters, instance method, subclass
questions.explanation: Override: same signature, instance method, not weaker access.

questions.question: Parent has static void m(); Child has static void m(); This is:
questions.explanation: Static methods are resolved at compile time; child hides parent.

questions.question: Which achieves abstraction by defining capability without implementation?
questions.explanation: Interfaces and abstract classes define contracts.

questions.question: What is printed?
questions.explanation: Virtual method invocation uses B's override.

questions.question: @Override annotation helps:
options: Catch mistakes when method does not actually override
questions.explanation: Compiler verifies an override exists in superclass.

questions.question: Can an interface extend another interface?
questions.explanation: Interfaces can extend multiple interfaces.

questions.question: Programming to interface List<String> list = new ArrayList<>(); benefits:
options: Allows swapping implementations with less client change
questions.explanation: Clients depend on abstraction, easing replacement.

questions.question: What does super.print() do inside a subclass method?
options: Invokes the superclass version of the method
questions.explanation: super targets superclass implementation.

questions.question: Immutable class pattern typically includes:
options: All fields private final, no setters, defensive copies
questions.explanation: Immutability: final fields, no mutators, safe copies.

questions.question: instanceof checks:
options: Whether an object is an instance of a type at runtime
questions.explanation: instanceof evaluates runtime type compatibility.

questions.question: Given interface Drawable { void draw(); } class Square implements Drawable { public void draw() { } } — new Square() is:
questions.explanation: Square is concrete and implements Drawable.

questions.question: Constructors are not inherited. Therefore a subclass must:
options: Invoke a superclass constructor (explicitly or via super())
questions.explanation: Chain to super constructor to initialize inherited state.

questions.question: Read-only property exposes:
questions.explanation: Getter without setter provides read-only access.

questions.question: Favor composition over inheritance when:
options: You need is-a subtype polymorphism always
You want to reuse behavior without tight coupling
questions.explanation: Composition reduces fragile base class coupling.

questions.question: What prints?
questions.explanation: Polymorphic call to Impl.val returns 7.

questions.question: Which members does a subclass inherit from a non-final parent?
options: Accessible non-private fields and methods (subject to visibility)
questions.explanation: Inherited members per visibility rules; constructors are not inherited.
